did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), to classify images of skin diseases (including melanoma, herpes, dermatitis, and psoriasis) for automated diagnosis. The methodology involves loading a dataset, preprocessing images, extracting features, and training a CNN model using TensorFlow/Keras to achieve a recognition rate of 90% or higher.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and implementation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for multi-class skin disease diagnosis. It details specific methodology including dataset loading, image preprocessing, feature extraction, and model training using TensorFlow/Keras. The authors report specific results, such as a recognition rate of 90% or higher on a dataset of 90 images (30 cases each for herpes, dermatitis, and psoriasis). Although it is published in a conference proceedings (RMKMATE), it contains original technical contributions and experimental validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Integrating Advanced Machine Learning Techniques for Multi-Class Skin Disease Diagnosis
journal: 2023 International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
brief_summary: This study develops an automated system for multi-class skin disease diagnosis using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify images of herpes, dermatitis, and psoriasis. The dataset consisted of 90 microscopic images, with thirty cases for each of the three conditions, standardized to 224 by 224 pixels. The methodology involved data preprocessing, feature extraction using color and texture analysis, and vertical image segmentation to enhance recognition accuracy. The model was implemented using TensorFlow and Keras on a Python-based platform. The system achieved a recognition rate of 90% or higher, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining color and texture features for skin disease identification. The authors conclude that this automated approach can assist in early detection, though it is not intended to replace human dermatological analysis.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin disease diagnosis (Herpes, Dermatitis, Psoriasis)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the focus is on the classification of common skin diseases such as herpes, dermatitis, and psoriasis. The model uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify images into these discrete diagnostic categories. The dataset consisted of 90 images with thirty cases for each of the three conditions. The system aims to distinguish between these specific disease labels.

datasets_used.name: Custom Microscopic Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset created by the authors consisting of microscopic images of skin lesions taken from real diagnostic cases. The images were prepared through fixation, selection, trimming, embedding, sectioning, and staining, then inspected under a microscope. All images were standardized to 224x224 pixels in RGB color space.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model (60 images total, 20 per class).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (30 images total: 20 test samples and 10 standard samples).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are reported as a recognition rate of 90% or higher.

Custom Microscopic Skin Disease Dataset.notes: The model was trained on 90 images (30 cases each for herpes, dermatitis, and psoriasis). The system utilized a combination of color and texture features along with vertical image segmentation. The recognition rate of 90% or higher was achieved by addressing the limitations of unidirectional recognition and enhancing accuracy through vertical image segmentation which yielded ten local vertical images.

architecture_details.implementation_notes: The model architecture consists of convolutional, pooling, ReLU, and fully connected layers. The trained model weights and architecture were saved in Keras file format (.h5). Vertical image segmentation was used to yield ten local vertical images as part of the feature extraction process.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing/downsizing to standardize dimensions to 224x224 pixels
Vertical image segmentation to yield ten local vertical images
Data cleaning (eliminating/filling missing values, locating/eliminating outliers, resolving discrepancies)
Data transformation (normalizing numerical data, encoding categorical data)
conclusions: The proposed automated system using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) successfully classifies skin diseases (herpes, dermatitis, and psoriasis) with a recognition rate of 90% or higher.
The integration of color and texture features addresses the limitations of unidirectional recognition, significantly improving accuracy.
Vertical image segmentation, which yields ten local vertical images, further enhances the accuracy of skin disease identification.
The automated method is designed to assist in the early detection of skin diseases, which is crucial for effective and less disfiguring treatments.
The authors explicitly state that the method does not aim to replace human analysis and intuition but rather serves as a supportive tool.

The study demonstrates that AI or Machine Learning methods can be effectively implemented for diagnosing skin diseases, potentially addressing the issue of limited access to medical care in remote areas.
important_remarks: Extremely small dataset size: The study uses only 90 microscopic images (30 per class) for a multi-class classification task, which is insufficient for robust deep learning model training and generalization.

Lack of external validation: The model was trained and tested on a single, proprietary dataset of microscopic images without any external validation on standard dermatological datasets (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000).

Non-standard imaging modality: The dataset consists of microscopic histopathology images rather than clinical dermoscopic or macroscopic images, limiting the clinical applicability for non-invasive skin disease diagnosis.
Absence of statistical rigor: The paper reports only a 'recognition rate of 90% or higher' without providing confusion matrices, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or confidence intervals.
No formal hypothesis testing: The study does not report p-values or perform statistical significance tests to validate the performance improvements.

Limited class scope: The model only distinguishes between herpes, dermatitis, and psoriasis, ignoring malignant conditions like melanoma in the experimental phase despite mentioning them in the introduction.
Potential data leakage or overfitting risk: With only 60 training images and 30 test images, the risk of overfitting is high, and the reported accuracy may not reflect real-world performance.
Deployment concerns: The system is described as a web-based application with GUI, but no details are provided on computational efficiency, inference time, or integration with clinical workflows.
